title: The internet is just HTTP
instruction:
Every time your browser loads a page, every time your app fetches data from an API — it's using HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol).

HTTP is a conversation:
- Your program sends a **request** (`GET /users`)
- The server sends back a **response** (status code + data)

Python's standard library has `urllib` for HTTP — no install needed. Let's try it.
starter:
import urllib.request
import json

with urllib.request.urlopen("https://httpbin.org/json") as response:
    data = json.loads(response.read())
    print(data["slideshow"]["title"])
    print(f"Status: {response.status}")
successMessage: You just made an HTTP request from Python! urllib fetched a JSON response, you parsed it, and pulled out a value. That's the foundation of every API call.

title: requests — urllib but human
instruction:
urllib works but is verbose. The `requests` library makes HTTP in Python actually enjoyable:

```python
import requests
response = requests.get(url)
data = response.json()
```

Three lines instead of ten.

Fetch a random user from a public API. The `.json()` method parses the JSON automatically.
starter:
import requests

# TODO: GET https://randomuser.me/api/ and print the first user's name
# data["results"][0]["name"]["first"] + " " + data["results"][0]["name"]["last"]

codeChecks.message: Use requests.get(url) to make the request.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: requests.get() + .json() — that's the whole API call. Requests is consistently one of the most downloaded Python packages in the world for good reason.

title: Query parameters
instruction:
APIs let you filter results with query parameters — the `?key=value` part of a URL.

Don't build the URL string manually — use the `params` dict:

```python
requests.get(url, params={"q": "python", "limit": 5})
```

Requests automatically URL-encodes them. Manual string building is messy and error-prone.

Search for posts by userId.
starter:
import requests

url = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"
# TODO: add params={"userId": 1} to filter posts by user
response = requests.get(url)
posts = response.json()
print(f"Found {len(posts)} posts")
if posts:
    print(f"First: {posts[0]['title'][:40]}")

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Filtered to only user 1's posts. Requests built the URL for you: .../posts?userId=1. Always use params= — it handles encoding, escaping, and multiple values automatically.

title: POST — sending data
instruction:
GET retrieves data. POST sends data — creating a new resource:

```python
requests.post(url, json={"key": "value"})
```

Use `json=` and requests automatically serialises to JSON and sets the Content-Type header.

Create a new post.
starter:
import requests

url = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"
new_post = {
    "title": "My First API Post",
    "body": "Learning Python HTTP the right way.",
    "userId": 1
}

# TODO: POST new_post to the URL and print the response status code and id
response = requests.get(url)  # BUG: should be POST

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Status 201 (Created) — the server acknowledged your new post. json= auto-serialises and sets Content-Type: application/json. Always use json= not data= when sending JSON.

title: Headers — send metadata
instruction:
HTTP headers carry metadata — authentication tokens, content type, user agent, language preferences.

The most important: `Authorization`:

```python
headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer your-token-here"}
requests.get(url, headers=headers)
```

Add an Authorization header to the request.
starter:
import requests

url = "https://httpbin.org/headers"
# TODO: add headers with Authorization and Accept fields
response = requests.get(url)
data = response.json()
print(data["headers"])

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Your headers arrived at the server. httpbin echoes back what it received. Authorization headers carry your API key or JWT token — the API uses them to identify you.

title: raise_for_status() — check for errors
instruction:
`requests.get()` does NOT raise an exception for HTTP error codes (404, 500, etc.) — it succeeds and returns the error response.

`response.raise_for_status()` converts HTTP errors to Python exceptions:
- 4xx → `HTTPError` (client errors: not found, unauthorised)
- 5xx → `HTTPError` (server errors)

Always call this after a request.
starter:
import requests

try:
    response = requests.get("https://httpbin.org/status/404")
    # TODO: add raise_for_status() here
    print("This should not print for a 404")
except requests.HTTPError as e:
    print(f"HTTP Error: {e.response.status_code}")

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 404 became a Python exception. Without raise_for_status(), your code would silently process error responses. Add it after every request — it's the safety net.

title: Sessions — reuse connections
instruction:
Every `requests.get()` opens a new TCP connection. For many requests to the same server, that's slow.

`requests.Session` reuses connections, sends headers automatically, and maintains cookies:

```python
with requests.Session() as session:
    session.headers["Authorization"] = "Bearer token"
    session.get(url1)  # uses shared headers and connection
    session.get(url2)  # reuses everything
```

Rewrite the three requests to use a Session with a shared User-Agent header.
hint:
with requests.Session() as session:
    session.headers["User-Agent"] = "MyApp/1.0"
    r1 = session.get(url1)
starter:
import requests

base_url = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com"

# TODO: use a Session for these three requests
r1 = requests.get(f"{base_url}/posts/1", headers={"User-Agent": "MyApp/1.0"})
r2 = requests.get(f"{base_url}/posts/2", headers={"User-Agent": "MyApp/1.0"})
r3 = requests.get(f"{base_url}/posts/3", headers={"User-Agent": "MyApp/1.0"})

for r in [r1, r2, r3]:
    print(r.json()["title"][:30])

codeChecks.message: Create a Session: with requests.Session() as session:
Set shared headers on the session: session.headers["User-Agent"]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Session reuses the TCP connection and sends User-Agent automatically. For any code that makes multiple requests to the same API, always use a Session — faster and cleaner.

title: Build an API client
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `JSONPlaceholderClient` class that:
- Has `base_url = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com"`
- Uses a `requests.Session` internally
- `get_post(id)` — returns a post dictionary
- `get_user_posts(user_id)` — returns list of posts for a user
- `create_post(title, body, user_id)` — creates a post and returns the created post

All methods should call raise_for_status().

codeChecks.message: Use a Session inside the class.

successMessage: A clean, reusable API client class. This is exactly how real Python SDKs are built — a class wrapping a Session, methods hiding the HTTP details. Chapter 23 done!
